places.name: Abu Dhabi

visits.text: Qasr Al Hosn, Abu Dhabi's oldest stone building, dates back to the 18th century—it even appears on the 1,000 AED banknote. Converted into a museum in 2018 after a decade of restoration, it traces the emirate's evolution from fishing village to oil metropolis. The adjacent Artisan House, with free admission, showcases UNESCO-listed Sadu weaving.
Mina Zayed fish market operates at full capacity from dawn. Fishermen unload hammour, kingfish, and barracuda. Arrive before 8am to witness informal auctions between restaurateurs.
Right next door, Mina Zayed fruit and vegetable market overflows with fresh dates, mangoes, and bulk spices. One of the few wholesale markets still accessible to individuals in the capital.

Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque features 82 Carrara marble domes, four 106-meter minarets, and accommodates 40,000 worshippers. Inside: the world's largest hand-knotted carpet—5,625 m², crafted by 1,200 Iranian artisans over 18 months. Construction commissioned in 1996 by the UAE's founding father, inaugurated in 2007. Allow at least 2 hours. Free guided tours at 10am, 11am, and 5pm (booking at szgmc.gov.ae).

Mangrove National Park covers 75% of the country's mangroves on Abu Dhabi island's eastern flank. Flamingos, herons, and dugongs inhabit these protected channels. The 2-km boardwalk has free entry. For a deeper experience, guided kayak tours last 2 hours (160 AED adult, departing from Eastern Mangroves Promenade). Best time: high tide between 1pm and 3pm.

activities.text: Abu Dhabi Corniche stretches 8 km along public beaches facing the Arabian Gulf. Evening stroll to watch the tower skyline illuminate over the water.
Sunset kayaking through mangrove channels—operators also offer night tours with underwater LED lighting to observe marine life.

places.name: Al Ain

visits.text: Al Ain Oasis spans 1,200 hectares—147,000 date palms of about a hundred varieties, irrigated by a 3,000-year-old falaj. UNESCO site since 2011, opened to the public in 2016. At the interactive eco-center, a 3D video explains how water flows through underground galleries. Rent a bike at the entrance: one hour suffices for the essentials.

Al Jahili Fort (1891), one of the UAE's largest forts, houses a permanent exhibition on Wilfred Thesiger—the British explorer who crossed the Rub al Khali on foot from Liwa in 1946 and 1948. Built to defend the oasis under orders from Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa.
Al Ain Camel Market, on the city's outskirts, is one of the last of its kind in the UAE. Open 6am to 7pm, traders negotiate young racing camels. Authentic scene, rarely frequented by tourists.

Jebel Hafeet reaches 1,249 m—the highest point in Abu Dhabi emirate, second-highest peak in the UAE. The 12-km scenic road to the summit has been ranked among the world's most beautiful by several automotive magazines. 360° view over the desert, Al Ain below, and the Omani border.

At the foot of Jebel Hafeet, over 500 beehive tombs dating from 3,200 to 2,600 BC mark the beginning of the Bronze Age in the region. UNESCO-listed since 2011. Danish archaeologists uncovered Mesopotamian pottery and copper objects here in 1959—proof of ancient maritime trade networks between Arabia, Mesopotamia, and the Indus Valley.

activities.text: Bike or walk through the shaded oasis pathways in late afternoon. Electric carts available for rent for less mobile visitors.
Green Mubazzarah at Jebel Hafeet's base: park with natural hot springs, lawns, and picnic areas. Good recovery stop after the climb, especially for families.

places.name: Al Aqah

visits.text: Al Bidiyah Mosque (circa 1446) is the oldest mosque still in service in the UAE. Built from earth bricks and gypsum over 53 m², it houses four unequal domes supported by a single central pillar—an engineering system without parallel in the region. Two watchtowers of the same era stand on the hill behind. Active mosque: modest dress required.
Dibba Bay and the Al Aqah coast: here the Hajar Mountains plunge directly into the Arabian Sea. Offshore rocks shelter coral colonies and parrotfish visible while snorkeling from the beach.

activities.text: Swimming and snorkeling on Al Aqah public beach—warm water, little current, sandy and rocky bottom. One of the most accessible spots to observe marine life on the east coast.

days_plan.itin_title: UAE Road Trip: Abu Dhabi, Liwa Desert, the East Coast, and Dubai in One Journey

map_keywords: Al Bidyah Mosque oldest UAE Fujairah
Al Aqah beach Fujairah Indian Ocean

visits.text: Ajman Museum occupies an 18th-century fort, one of the few well-preserved on the Gulf coast. Its rooms trace Bedouin life, pearl fishing, and the history of this emirate—the smallest in the UAE.
Sharjah's Central Souq (Blue Souk), recognizable by its blue ceramic towers, is one of the region's largest craft markets—carpets, pottery, jewelry, calligraphy. Ten minutes' drive from Ajman.

Dubai Creek has separated Deira from Bur Dubai since the 19th century. The Al Fahidi district (1787) is the city's oldest—its wind towers, coral alleyways, and museum in the fort tell the story of Dubai before oil. The abra crossing costs 1 AED and takes 5 minutes.

Deira's Gold Souk displays over 25 tons of gold in shop windows across some 350 boutiques—bracelets, necklaces, and monumental pieces regulated by the state. Five minutes' walk away, the Spice Souk perfumes the air with saffron, cinnamon, and incense: the trip's best olfactory souvenir.

activities.text: Coastal road from Sharjah to Dubai along the corniches—photo stops at coves and seafront parks.
Abra ride on the Creek at sunset—the sky turns orange over Bur Dubai's historic facades while dhows loaded with goods return to port.

days_plan.itin_title: UAE Road Trip: Abu Dhabi, Liwa Desert, the East Coast, and Dubai in One Journey
UAE Road Trip: Abu Dhabi, Liwa Desert, the East Coast, and Dubai in One Journey

places.name: Fujairah

visits.text: Hatta Dam forms a turquoise lake wedged into the Hajar Mountains, 130 km from Al Ain via the E66. Built in the 1990s, its red rock shores plunging into calm water offer one of the UAE's most unexpected panoramas. Kayaks for rent on-site (60 AED/single kayak, unlimited time), open from 7am.

Al Bithnah Fort (1735) controlled the only natural pass through the Hajar Mountains from Fujairah's coast to the interior—Wadi Ham. Rectangular fort of stone, coral, and mud-brick, restored in 2012. The view from the upper terraces over the valley and palm groves warrants the stop. Free entry.

Fujairah Fort, perched on a rocky outcrop at the city's entrance, is one of the oldest on the east coast—some sections date to the 16th century. View over the Arabian Sea from the ramparts. At its feet, ruins of the old Fujairah village abandoned in the 1960s.
Khor Fakkan, a Sharjah enclave framed by limestone cliffs dropping into the sea, deserves a 30-minute detour north of Fujairah. The seafront amphitheatre was carved directly from the rock.

activities.text: Drive down the E89 from Hatta toward Fujairah through Hajar Mountain gorges—winding road between ochre cliffs and dry wadis. Photo stops on the bends.
Fujairah's public beaches line the Arabian Sea for several kilometers—calmer water and less crowded than the Gulf. Swimming or snorkeling depending on conditions.

places.name: Liwa Mezairaa

visits.text: From Abu Dhabi, the E11 then E45 crosses sabkhas (salt flats) before plunging into the oasis. Along a 100-km east-west arc, about fifty villages have cultivated dates since the 15th century. Liwa is the birthplace of the Al Nahyan family—the future rulers of Abu Dhabi departed from here in 1793 to settle on the coast.

Liwa's forts, scattered along the E90 road, once protected tribal farms and wells. Most remain on private property—drive along the route and photograph from the track. The dune ridges flanking them ignite at sunset.

Tal Moreeb rises 300 meters with a 50° face—one of the highest and steepest dunes in the Rub al Khali. The paved road from Mezairaa (25 km) crosses the most spectacular dunes on the Arabian Peninsula. Photo stops at every bend. This desert served as backdrop for Dune (2021) and Star Wars: The Force Awakens (2015).

activities.text: Sunset over the dunes towering above Mezairaa. From parking zones on the road toward Moreeb, the panorama is breathtaking in the hour before dusk.
Walk in the dunes at the foot of Tal Moreeb—15 minutes on foot from the parking area puts you alone in the sand. Camel herders sometimes pass along the track.

places.name: Mleiha

visits.text: Mleiha Archaeological Centre sits at the heart of a desert landscape UNESCO-listed in 2025 (Faya Palaeolithic Landscape). Excavations have unearthed traces of Homo sapiens dating back 130,000 years—among the earliest evidence of human presence outside Africa in the region. The museum is built around an Umm Al Nar culture tomb (Bronze Age, ~2,300 BC) still in situ.

Fossil Rock (Jebel Mleiha), a few kilometers from the centre, is a limestone formation riddled with 90-million-year-old marine fossils—the ancient floor of the Tethys Sea. Accessible by 4x4 or on foot from the parking area (45 min round trip). Panoramic view of the transition from beige desert to orange dunes.

activities.text: The site lends itself to stargazing: 65 km from Sharjah, light pollution is nearly zero. The centre organizes non-exclusive observation evenings (book on-site).

places.name: Ras Al Khaimah

visits.text: Dhayah Fort, the last hilltop fort still standing in the UAE, dominates the plain from its rocky spur after a 200-step climb. Built in the early 19th century, it was the last bastion during the 1819 British expedition. Panoramic view of mangroves, sea, and mountains.

Al Jazirah Al Hamra, a pearling village abandoned in 1968, is the only preserved example of a pre-oil coastal village in the entire Gulf. Its houses of coral blocks and palm wood were mass-abandoned by the Al Zaabi tribe—who received Sheikh Zayed's offer to relocate to Abu Dhabi. The alleyways are open to visit; do not enter fragile buildings.

activities.text: Walk along Ras Al Khaimah's coastal mangroves in late afternoon—the low light highlights the mangrove roots.
